text: Oh, not again. Shelby Oaks is a 2025 American horror movie written and directed by YouTube movie critic Chris Stockman in his feature film debut, starring Camille Sullivan as Mia, Sarah Durn as her sister Riley, Brendan Ston as Mia's husband, Robert, and Robin Bartlett as a creepy old lady. A continuation of an online series of fictional found footage videos about a paranormal investigation team named the Paranormal Paranoids. The film follows a woman named Mia who's

determined to find her sister Riley who went missing while investigating the mysterious abandoned town of Shelby Oaks. There was always going to be a lot of conversation around an online personality like Chris Stuckman trying his hand at film making and the involvement of a respected industry figure like Mike Flanigan. But really, I didn't find that it impacted my experience of the movie too much. This film features camera angles performances, and dialogue from actors

who say words to each other. And overall, I think Chris did a good job with this movie, and I'm looking forward to seeing more projects from him in the future. And if you like this, you can click right here and get drinkerized.

>> Damn,, man., I, just, had, the, wildest, dream. Anyway, what the hell am I supposed to be talking about today? Oh, yeah. Shelby Oaks. Now, reviewing a film like this is a bit of a tricky prospect because whether you consider it a good movie is going to depend a lot on what kind of standard you want to measure it against. As the first feature film by a YouTube movie critic that's decided to throw his hat in the ring and try his hand at film making, well, it's pretty [ __ ] good

actually. And whatever else I end up saying in this review, I do want to congratulate Chris Stockman on what he's achieved here. Bringing your artistic vision to life with a project of this size and scope is no small feat. and having the balls to be the man in the arena instead of just another cynical critic who never tries anything of their own is a pretty admirable thing. And whatever issues people might have with him as a movie critic sorry, film celebrator, I think he

deserves a lot of respect for putting himself out there like this. All that being said, this is still a theatrically released movie that people are expected to part with their hard-earned cash to watch, and I'm just guessing here, but I would assume Chris would want it to be judged as such. And that being the case I should at least start with the positives. It's better than Rogue Elements. That's for [ __ ] sure. >> Fake, laugh, hiding, real, pain. >> On, a, technical, level,, I, think, Shelby

Oaks is actually pretty good. What can I say? It looks like a legit movie. Whoever was DP on this thing did a solid job framing up their shots, setting the lighting properly, and creating a nice sense of dynamic movement. All the usual locations you'd expect in a horror flick are present and correct. abandoned buildings, creepy cabins in the woods and underground tunnels, and they all look pretty decent. There's even a few pretty effective jump scares in there.

Nothing that's going to have you soiling your pants or anything, but enough to prove that Stuckman understands how to draw out the tension and pull the trigger at just the right moments. Performances are generally fine, I guess. Nobody goes into low-budget horror movies looking for Oscar winning acting, but I think the cast does the best they can with the material they're given. Camille Sullivan carries the bulk of the movie with a solid, if slightly unspectacular, performance as Mia. She's

a bit anemic at times, especially near the start, but she definitely seems to grow in confidence as the story progresses. All of this is my way of saying that if Chris Stockman's goal with Shelby Oaks was to prove that he's a competent director with potential to go on to better things, then mission accomplished. That being said, Shelby Oaks is a movie with problems, and most of them stem from the scripts. The biggest of which is that it can't seem to decide what it is. So, it tries to be

a bit of everything. And the result is a cluttered and tonally mismatched narrative that lacks focus. It's like someone went to him and said, "Hey Chris, what kind of horror movie are you going to make?" And his answer was swift and decisive. Yes. Found footage horror mockumentary investigations, narrative drama, childhood trauma manifesting in later life, abandoned towns, abandoned prisons, abandoned theme parks, demonic possession, death cults, serial killers

cabins in the woods, creepy old ladies monsters, following a trail of clues and even fractured family dynamics. Shut up. It's all crammed in there somehow, and it's just too [ __ ] much. The script feels like a Frankenstein's monster stitched together from pieces of other better stories, unable to settle on a strong central arc that it can develop properly. Then there's the pacing. The first 20 minutes are basically an extended info dump giving you Riley's

entire life story and everything leading up to her disappearance. It goes on forever, and I can't shake the feeling that it's all a bit unnecessary because apart from being this vague objective that Mia's searching for, Riley barely factors into the narrative at all. The second act, though, is where the plot drags, and that's saying something for a film that's only 90 minutes long. Through circumstances that are a bit too silly to go into, Mia inherits an oldfashioned cassette tape featuring

unseen footage of her sister's disappearance that's helpfully got Shelby Oaks written on it. So, you might think it would make sense to head straight there and pick up the trail. Instead, she goes into private investigator mode and visits basically every location and character except Shelby Oaks. The bulk of the second act is literally just Mia wandering around and looking at things with a flashlight. Tree carvings, messages, photo albums video recordings, burn marks, mold

rust, books. It's never ending, and most of it doesn't really progress the story either. It's just there to eat up time. A prime example of this narrative blow is Keith David's scene where he's literally just there to deliver 2 minutes of exposition and is never seen again. I mean, I love Keith David as an actor. Everyone loves Keith David, but the way he delivers his lines here, it feels like he just showed up on the day read the script like five minutes before

shooting, collected his paycheck, and left. I mean, if you've got a guy of that caliber on set, use him. And as for the finale, well, this is where most horror movies tend to come undone because the moment you understand the nature of the threat, it stops being scary and so you have to rely on strong character payoffs to carry the day. Shelby Oaks definitely takes a crack at this, but the way it plays out with one character getting blasted through a window and eaten by hell hounds that

just conveniently show up, well, it felt more amusing than scary. Ultimately Shelby Oaks is kind of what I expected after all the hype and speculation. Neither an artistic triumph or an embarrassing disaster, but instead a flawed and cautious kind of movie from a filmmaker who's still finding his feet in the industry. There's nothing in it that you haven't seen a million times before and probably seen done better. And at the end of the day, the worst I can say about Shelby Oaks is that it's
just another horror movie. Unfortunately, the best I can say about it is that it's also just another horror movie. Anyway, that's all I've got for today. Go away
